title: Minimize access to secrets

input_row.title: Minimize access to secrets
input_row.description: The Kubernetes API stores secrets, which may be service account tokens for the Kubernetes API or credentials used by workloads in the cluster. Access to these secrets should be restricted to the smallest possible group of users to reduce the risk of privilege escalation.
input_row.rationale: Inappropriate access to secrets stored within the Kubernetes cluster can allow for an attacker to gain additional access to the Kubernetes cluster or external resources whose credentials are stored as secrets. Impact: Care should be taken not to remove access to secrets to system components which require this for their operation
input_row.audit: Review the users who have get, list or watch access to secrets objects in the Kubernetes API.
input_row.remediation: Where possible, remove get, list and watch access to secret objects in the cluster. Default Value: By default in a kubeadm cluster the following list of principals have get privileges on secret objects CLUSTERROLEBINDING                                    SUBJECT TYPE            SA-NAMESPACE cluster-admin                                         system:masters Group system:controller:clusterrole-aggregation-controller  clusterrole- aggregation-controller  ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:expand-controller                   expand-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:generic-garbage-collector           generic-garbage- collector           ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:namespace-controller                namespace-controller ServiceAccount  kube-system system:controller:persistent-volume-binder            persistent-volume- binder            ServiceAccount  kube-system system:kube-controller-manager                        system:kube-controller- manager      User
input_row.impact: Care should be taken not to remove access to secrets to system components which require this for their operation

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_rbac_secret_access_minimized

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Query all ClusterRole, Role, ClusterRoleBinding, and RoleBinding objects in the cluster using the Kubernetes API or kubectl.
  - Example commands:
    - `kubectl get clusterrole -o yaml`
    - `kubectl get role --all-namespaces -o yaml`
    - `kubectl get clusterrolebinding -o yaml`
    - `kubectl get rolebinding --all-namespaces -o yaml`
- For each ClusterRole and Role, inspect the `rules` field for any entries where:
  - `resources` includes `secrets`
  - `verbs` includes any of: `get`, `list`, or `watch`
- Enumerate all subjects (users, groups, service accounts) bound to these roles via ClusterRoleBindings and RoleBindings.
- Output a list of all principals (users, groups, service accounts) with `get`, `list`, or `watch` access to `secrets` in any namespace.
- Pass Criteria: Only system components and service accounts required for cluster operation (as per CIS default list) have these permissions. Any additional users/groups/service accounts with these permissions should be flagged for review.
- Example kubectl query to find roles with secret access:
  - `kubectl get clusterrole,role --all-namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.rules[]? | .resources[]? == "secrets" and (.verbs[] | IN("get","list","watch"))) | .metadata.name'`
- Example to enumerate bindings:
  - Cross-reference the above roles with `kubectl get clusterrolebinding,rolebinding --all-namespaces -o yaml` to list all subjects granted these roles.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.9.0 PDF
